summary: When hiring an engineering manager, companies should always give internal candidates first consideration before looking externally, since effective management requires deep knowledge of the tech stack, existing relationships, and team trust. The post argues that management should be treated as a lateral career change rather than a promotion, and that the best engineer is rarely the right choice for manager.
key_points: Internal candidates should always get first crack at management openings due to their existing relationships and technical context
Management is a career change, not a promotion — decouple it from leadership and status
Don't promote your best engineer to manager; there's no positive correlation between engineering skill and management skill
Engineers need at least 5-7 years of hands-on experience before considering management
The team's opinion of a proposed manager matters more than leadership's opinion
money_quotes: There is no correlation that I've observed between someone's engineering skills and management skills – no positive correlation, at any rate.
You won't be doing them any favors by asking them to switch careers earlier in their tenure, and you might well be inflicting permanent damage on their career.
You should never inflict a manager on a team who doesn't want to receive them in that role, no matter how qualified they seem on paper.
Hiring isn't about finding the 'best person', it's about crafting the strongest team.
Being a 'really good person' is not enough to do the job; 'good people' inflict immense harm every day without meaning to.
key_insight: Engineering management should be treated as a lateral career change that prioritizes internal candidates with strong relationships and team trust, not as a promotion for your best technical performer.
